data.title: TQQQ: A Correction Could Be A Blessing In Disguise (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
In the very long run, defined here as a period of 10 years or more, I am bullish on the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) and its super-aggressive, leveraged ETF version, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ (TQQQ)—the subject of today's article. For this reason, I am selfishly hoping for a deeper correction in the equities market than what we have seen in the past five weeks, believing that this would increase the likelihood of oversized returns in the next several years if one deploys a sensible risk management strategy (something that, in my view, is a must-have when using leverage).

A bull hoping for a pullback may sound counterintuitive. Perhaps the obvious explanation is that corrections uncover the potential for more profitable buy-the-dip trades. While this is ultimately what lies at the center of my "bad is good" argument, today I am proposing a more structured way to think about sidestepping a potential correction and riding an eventual recovery in a more systematic but very simple way.

About TQQQ

Before diving any deeper, let's briefly talk about the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, a fund that seeks to produce three times the daily returns of the Nasdaq 100 index. It does so by not only holding the individual components of the benchmark but also by being long a series of index swap contracts (see holdings for more details).

On the minus side, TQQQ comes with a series of risks. The most important of them is that 3x leverage substantially increases the volatility and drawdown potential to levels that most investors would not be comfortable with—which is why, in my view, TQQQ is best held alongside cash and/or "anti-fragile" asset classes and instruments, such as VIX-linked (VIXY, VIXM), low-beta long-short (BTAL), and managed futures (CTA, KMLM) funds. The other key risk is volatility drag, which means that TQQQ could produce substantial losses over more than a day even if QQQ moves sideways or slightly up (find out more about volatility drag from the SEC).

On the plus side, TQQQ does not always have to be treated as a day-trading instrument. If blended properly with cash to reduce its risk profile, a portfolio that is invested one-third in TQQQ and two-thirds in cash can perform roughly in line with a simple QQQ holding over a multi-year period, as depicted in the chart below. This means that, yes, a leveraged ETF can be used as a tool by investors interested in a decade-plus investment horizon, provided that the tool is used properly (i.e., not as a gambling instrument).

With Risk Management, Bad is Good

Those who follow me know that I have written plenty about using moving averages (I know, technical analysis, yuck!) as a risk management tool that can increase risk-adjusted and even absolute returns over the long run. At a very high level, here is how it works, as I stated in one of my recent articles:

The idea is simple: own an asset when its short-term moving average is above its long-term moving average, and sell (or don’t own) the asset when the opposite is true.

As the Nasdaq 100 had corrected as much as 12% from the all-time highs right before the end of March, its 22-trading-day moving average dipped below the 200-day equivalent on April 1, as the chart below depicts. Based on the trading rule above, this would have been the time to sell the Nasdaq or its TQQQ cousin-on-steroids.

Notice that, in the ultra-short term, the decision seems to have been ill-timed, since the Nasdaq has returned about +2% in the first couple of trading sessions of April, and TQQQ has been up about +6%. But the focus here is on long-term trading diligence, not on making a quick buck before lunchtime.

A few things could happen next:

  • The Nasdaq 100 recovers fast, in V-shaped style, resulting in the moving-average trading approach forcing an investor to miss out on the first leg of the rebound but eventually getting the investor back on track to produce positive returns (a seemingly bad outcome relative to the benchmark, but certainly not a terrible one)
  • The Nasdaq 100 bounces up and down but moves generally sideways, possibly causing the dreaded "whipsaw effect" in which a trader buys and sells the Nasdaq a few times and treads water in the process (again, a bad outcome that is not necessarily terrible)
  • The Nasdaq 100 keeps declining and keeps searching for a bottom (relatively speaking, a great outcome for the investor using the risk management tactic described)

Allow me to focus on the last bullet point above, which is what I would shamelessly hope to see next. Below, I simulate what would happen to a $1,000 investment if:

  1. I held one-half TQQQ and one-half cash rebalanced daily for simplicity (one could work with the full 3x leverage factor, i.e., 100% allocation to TQQQ, but the position seems too speculative for my taste)
  2. I exited the position when the short-term moving average dipped below the long-term moving average
  3. My position produced returns that are in line with the price action from the following bearish periods, from the time that the moving average strategy triggered a sell until the Nasdaq reclaimed all-time highs again:
    • 2000-2003 dot-com crash (all-time high reclaimed only in 2015)
    • 2022 interest rate shock (all-time high reclaimed in late 2023)

The charts below depict how the strategy would have performed in both cases (the blue line is the moving-average approach, while the orange line is QQQ).

The first chart shows annual returns of 9% per year over the entire 15-year period, with only very brief moments of decline below the initial $1,000 capital deployed, against less than 2% annual gains in the Nasdaq 100 and sharp drawdowns below the initial investment levels. The second chart shows lavish annual returns of 26% per year over the shorter two-year period, against mid-single-digit returns in the Nasdaq 100.

Notice how I purposely left out the 2008 Great Financial Crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 bear. Both are classic cases of too many buy and sell signals being triggered, which would have resulted in the 1/2 TQQQ investment with the moving average strategy producing roughly the same returns as a simple QQQ buy-and-hold approach. This is evidence that aggressively owning a leveraged fund while managing the downside risk during bearish periods does not always produce superior returns in the longer term (i.e., don't always expect easy gains served on a platter).

Key Takeaways

I don't have a strong view on whether the Nasdaq 100 will recover from its (relatively mild) Iran War correction right away or whether we will see lower lows before the index eventually reclaims all-time highs. But using a moving-average approach applied to a leveraged ETF like TQQQ could prove to be a win-win strategy today.

Should the Nasdaq rebound quickly, an investor staying on the sidelines today could miss the bullish jolt in the next few days or a couple of weeks but would likely eventually catch up due to the leveraged exposure (assume stock prices go up over time, not down). Should the Nasdaq dip further instead, the same investor would likely produce market-beating returns in the immediate term (i.e., flattish vs. negative returns in QQQ) while being exposed to the possibility of buying the index at lower, more attractive valuations when the benchmark finally begins to recover.

data.title: BlackRock Inflation Protected Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -0.18% (Institutional shares) and -0.17% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • The fund underperformed its benchmark as inflation expectations declined and the U.S. nominal yield curve generally steepened.
  • We slightly increased the long exposure to out-of-benchmark Consumer Price Index basket-linked assets, while we slightly reduced the long position in five-year breakevens (the difference in yield between nominal U.S. Treasuries and Treasury inflation-protected securities with comparable maturities). The fund held a relative value position in UK breakevens and maintained a yield curve-flattening position in Japanese nominal rates.

Contributors

Detractors
The tactical long position in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was a notable contributor as spreads tightened due to Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts and a steepening of the yield curve (where long-term yields increase faster than short-term rates). The rental inflation-linked and credit strategies benefited from positive carry (income) and a generally supportive fixed income backdrop. The fund's exposure to five-year U.S. inflation breakevens performed weakly as inflation expectations declined, moving against our view, while U.S. rates also detracted. The yield curve generally steepened, and the fund's "flattener" (positioning that seeks to benefit from a flattening of the yield curve) weighed on performance, though some of that drag was reversed after we shifted into a "steepener" position in December. The Japanese rates position detracted modestly as the Japanese yield curve steepened overall, against our view that it would flatten.

Further insight

We slightly decreased the allocation to agency MBS in October and November, while we added back some exposure in December given expectations that spreads would tighten. The fund maintained its modest long position in Brazilian local rates, as well as a long spread exposure to Mexico. The countries offer compelling real yields, supported by improved economic backdrop, increased demand for local bonds by foreign investors, and accommodative central banks. During the first half of the quarter, we expected the yield curve to flatten between the two-year and 10-year parts. However, we switched to a yield curve steepener toward the end of the quarter, preferring to hold a long position in the two-year and five-year portions relative to the 30-year part.

★★★★ Morningstar Overall™

Institutional shares rated against 138 US Fund Inflation-Protected Bond Funds, as of 12/31/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. 11

Portfolio management

Johan Sjogren, Harrison Segall,Russell Brownback

Top 10 holdings (%)

Umbs 30yr Tba(reg A) 4.78
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.87507/15/2035 4.51
Treasury (cpi) Note 2.12501/15/2035 4.33
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.62504/15/2030 3.98
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.87507/15/2034 3.84
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.7501/15/2034 3.63
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.37507/15/2033 3.38
Treasury (cpi) Note 1.12501/15/2033 3.37
Treasury (cpi) Note 0.62507/15/2032 3.33
Treasury (cpi) Note 0.12501/15/2031 3.33

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in inflation-indexed bonds of varying maturities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. governments, their agencies or instrumentalities, and U.S. and non-U.S. corporations.

Average annual total returns (%) as of 12/31/25

4Q25(not annualized) YTD(not annualized) 1 Year 3 Year 5 Years 10 Years
Institutional -0.18 6.85 6.85 4.13 1.11 3.02
Investor A (Without Sales Charge) -0.17 6.65 6.65 3.88 0.86 2.76
Investor A (With Sales Charge) -4.17 2.39 2.39 2.48 0.04 2.34
Morningstar US Fund Inflation-Protected Bond Category Avg. -0.01 6.86 6.86 3.68 0.43 2.71
U.S. TIPS1 0.13 7.01 7.01 4.23 1.13 3.09

Expenses for Institutional shares: Total 1.27%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 1.09%. For Investor A shares: Total 1.57%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 1.34%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund's most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to Advisor Center Resources for US Financial Advisors | BlackRock for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.34%; for Investor A shares: 0.59%. Index returns are for illustrative purposes only. Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset-backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. government agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government. Non-investment-grade debt securities (high-yield/junk bonds) may be subject to greater market fluctuations, risk of default or loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. International investing involves risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened in emerging markets. If the index measuring inflation falls, the principal value of inflation-indexed bonds will go down and the interest payable will be reduced. Any increase in the principal amount will be considered taxable ordinary income. Repayment of the original bond principal upon maturity (adjusted for inflation) is guaranteed for U.S. Treasury inflation-indexed bonds. For bonds that do not provide a guarantee, the adjusted principal value repaid at maturity may be less than the original principal. The fund may use derivatives to hedge investments or seek enhanced returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management team as of December 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The Bloomberg U.S. TIPS Index comprises inflation-protected public obligations of the U.S. Treasury. †† The Morningstar Rating™ for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled US Fund Inflation-Protected Bond funds over the following time periods: 138 in the last 3 years, 131 in the last 5 years and 108 in the last 10 years. With respect to these US Fund Inflation-Protected Bond funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 4 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

©2026 BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved. BLACKROCK is a trademark of BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All other trademarks are those of their respective owners.

Prepared by BlackRock Investments, LLC, member FINRA.

Not FDIC Insured • May Lose Value • No Bank Guarantee

01/26 – Inflation Protected Bond Fund

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Iovance Biotherapeutics: Positioned For Multi-Year Growth, Buy The Pullback
data.description:
Shares of Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) have lost nearly two-thirds of their value since Amtagvi was approved in February 2024. On the other hand, shares were up by as much as 80% YTD mid-March due to increasing enthusiasm for Amtagvi's commercial prospects in melanoma along with accelerating clinical momentum plus improving margins (transitioning from science project to viable business).

Current pullback puts YTD gain at +20% and is likely due in part to aspiring competitor Replimune (REPL), with the April 10th PDUFA date coming up for RP1 in melanoma (combined with Bristol's PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor nivolumab).

While it's possible IOVA could experience a gap down if RP1 gets approved, I think bears and skeptics are missing the forest for the trees as the overall story continues to get better.

Chart

Figure 1: IOVA daily chart (Source: Finviz)

In the chart above, we can see shares were rangebound for much of the past year (stuck at around $2.50). In late February, they jumped on the Q4 update and ran to $5.50 before experiencing a gap down followed by a slow decline to $3.30.

My suggestion for readers who like this name is to accumulate weakness in the near term with an eye toward sales growth and pipeline progress during the 2026 to 2028 time period.

Select Recent Developments

Real-world results for Amtagvi were shared in an oral presentation at ASTCT and CIBMTR annual meetings. Physician-assessed ORR was 44% with a 73% disease control rate in 41 patients. Of note, responses were higher when patients had two or fewer prior lines of therapy (52% ORR). Both of these figures are significantly better than the 31% ORR reported from the pivotal trial and could boost uptake in less heavily pretreated melanoma patients as physician enthusiasm rises.

Figure 2: 5-year data from pivotal melanoma data showcases impressive durability that competitors will have to match (Source: corporate slides)

In the Q4 report released February 24th, the theme was increasing demand for Amtagvi driving sales growth, improved margins, and accelerating pipeline momentum. Q4 revenue was $87M ($65M Amtagvi, $22M Proleukin), representing 30% growth over the prior quarter. Q4 gross margin from cost of sales was 50% (increased cost optimization). Full-year 2025 total product revenue of $264M slightly exceeded guidance.

With $303M of cash on hand, management guided for operational runway into Q3 2027 (sounds like a secondary offering is likely in the next quarter or two to extend that). Encouragingly, they continue to guide for continued improvement in operating expenses, cost of sales, and gross margin throughout 2026 and into 2027.

Referral trends toward treating earlier-line melanoma patients are strengthening, with adoption increasing due to real-world response rates (captures the true value-add of Amtagvi better than the original pivotal trial results, which were obfuscated by treating later-line patients). ATC network is growing nicely, with further acceleration expected, and community ATCs driving demand this year and next. Specialty distribution channel was introduced as another option for ATCs to purchase Amtagvi.

As for geographic expansion, regulatory reviews are underway in the UK and Australia, with potential approvals in 1H 26 (Switzerland to follow in 1H 27). EMA resubmission is also expected this year.

Updated data for NSCLC is expected at a major medical meeting this year along with completion of enrollment for a pivotal trial (BLA submission to follow with potential launch in 2H 27). This is important, given the opportunity is much larger than the initial advanced melanoma market.

A new indication was uncovered with UPS and DDLPS (sarcoma subtypes) representing another path to market (encouraging 50% ORR in the first six evaluable patients). A single-arm registrational study in previously treated patients will get underway in Q2 2026, followed by engagement with FDA on a faster path to approval (I like how this is being prioritized). Devil's advocate, I don't like how we didn't hear anything on endometrial cancer data (makes me wonder if this is a "bait and switch" type scenario).

Enrollment is accelerating for the phase 3 frontline melanoma study for Amtagvi, and next-gen products (PD-1 inactivated and IL-2 analog) continue in earlier-stage studies. IL-12 tethered TIL is slated to enter phase 1/2 study 2H 26. Several investigator-sponsored studies are ongoing for new solid tumor indications (perhaps additional fast paths to market will be discovered, as was the case for sarcoma). This latter point is an underappreciated aspect of the Iovance story, in my opinion.

Focusing on the sarcoma opportunity, there are over 3,000 patients in the US and 5,000 in Europe with the UPS and DDLPS subtypes (high-grade & aggressive). Unmet need here is unfortunately very high, with less than 5% ORR for 2nd-line treatment, median PFS of 2-3 months, and median overall survival of 9 to 10 months (contrast to 50% ORR for Amtagvi). I see this as a blockbuster opportunity, but let's peg peak sales at $500M to be conservative. It should also be relatively feasible to expand out into additional sarcoma subtypes from there. It sounds like the FDA would be equally (or even more) amenable to single-arm studies supporting accelerated approval for both UPS and DDLPS as well as additional subtypes (I don't see why related indications wouldn't work based on initial data).

Here are a few nuggets from the conference call that stood out to me:

  • Management highlighted 30% revenue growth with the best-ever 50% margin from cost of sales.
  • They are projecting $1B+ peak sales for Amtagvi + Proleukin, and enrollment volumes are accelerating within their ATC network.
  • Operational improvements are evident throughout the entire Amtagvi treatment journey, from finding patients to manufacturing product to infusion.
  • Long-term vision is massive expansion of their TIL platform into new indications (supporting growth for years to come).

Figure 3: Market opportunity across solid tumors (Source: corporate slides)

  • They are in the early stages of penetrating the community market, which will unlock significant potential (could result in better referrals to melanoma doctors).
  • Real-world data is resonating with medical oncologists, leading to tissue procurement earlier on in the patient journey, including for specific subtypes (i.e., those with BRAF mutation before they suffer health declines).
  • Per management, Amtagvi has the potential to reach over 30,000 patients annually in the previously treated advanced melanoma market alone as global expansion continues (still sounds incredibly rosy to me).
  • Interestingly, the NSCLC opportunity is 7 times larger, with 50,000 addressable patients for peak sales of $10 billion in the US alone (at the current valuation, even achieving 5% of that number would be a significant achievement). What's cool here is this launch would be different, as the heavy lifting (building out the ATC network) has already been accomplished for melanoma. They could leverage the existing ATC network (won't have to build it out again).
  • Again, management confirmed that updated data for NSCLC will be presented at a major medical meeting this year
  • For sarcoma, based on prior approvals in various subtypes, the pivotal study size could be just 30 to 60 patients (more likely ~40 patients). I like how efficient this sounds in terms of capital spend and timeline.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, at a $1.1 billion enterprise value on the pullback, I think that shares of Iovance are currently being discounted due to such concerns as competition or perhaps the need to access funding in the near term.

The company clearly fits our selection criteria for the Core Biotech portfolio, with Amtagvi sales set to pick up in coming quarters and over a multi-year timeframe along with improving margins. Pipeline momentum is also encouraging, with pivotal lung cancer data this year and possible approval next year, then the newest indication of sarcoma to follow (potentially endometrial cancer after that).

Risks to the thesis should not be overlooked, such as dilutive secondary offering in the next quarter or two. Shares could also be pressured if competitor RP1 from Replimune receives approval soon, though I again note this therapy is being combined with PD-1, and some doctors are still skeptical about PD-1 retreatment (versus employing an entirely novel mechanism of action). Lung cancer data could worsen with higher number of patients or longer follow-up, as could sarcoma, which is starting from a very small base of patients from the initial readout. Competition from much more convenient or efficacious next-generation treatments is something to keep an eye on as well, such as Immatics (IMTX) with interim and final phase 3 data for cell therapy candidate Anzu-cel this year and BLA submission slated for 2027.

data.title: Wabtec: Full Steam Ahead
data.description:
Shares of Wabtec (WAB) have been trading strong, currently changing hands at just over $250 per share, within imminent reach of all-time highs. Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies benefits from continued organic growth, solid M&A efforts, and a strong order intake, firing on all cylinders here.

On top of strong current operating momentum, which has been going on for a couple of years, further growth is seen in sales and margins in the coming years. This provides a solid roadmap for the company to execute upon and even enjoy the ride. However, with shares trading near the all-time highs, for good reasons, quite some good news has been priced in here, with shares trading around 25 times forward earnings.

This makes me appreciative of the performance of the business, yet I am hard-pressed to see appeal here, although I am seriously considering serious setbacks here. My regard for Wabtec has only been increasing. After I was constructive on the shares in the fall at levels just below the $200 mark, shares have risen by a quarter ever since, killing the immediate appeal.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Good News Show

In February, Wabtec announced the closure of the $960 million acquisition of Dellner-Couplers, a Swedish-based firm whose acquisition was announced early last year.

Other good news was reported on the order front. In February, the company reported a $670 million deal with CSX (CSX) to upgrade a fleet of 100 new Evolution Series locomotives and 50 modernized locomotives. That news came days after the company signed a $1.2 billion deal with Union Pacific (UNP) to modernize its AC4400 locomotives. In fact, a third order was reported this quarter, with Wabtec announcing a smaller $386 million deal with the New York MTA back in January. Combined, these orders total $2.2 billion—that's a substantial number, almost equivalent to a quarter's worth of sales.

Meanwhile, Wabtec reported solid results for the year 2025 in the middle of February. Full-year sales rose by nearly 8% to $11.2 billion, with fourth-quarter sales seen up nearly 15% to levels just shy of $3.0 billion, with revenues trending near $12 billion each year.

Full-year GAAP operating margins rose by more than a point to 18% of sales. This is even after a $300 million amortization charge, with adjusted margins seen in the low twenties. Adjusted earnings rose by 19% to $8.97 per share, with this adjustment looking fair in relation to a $6.83 per share number based on GAAP accounting, really driven by amortization charges.

Net debt was reported at $4.75 billion, that all being very reasonable with EBITDA seen near $2.5 billion for the year, as leverage is seen just below 2 times, that ahead of the Dellner-Couplers deal closing in the first quarter.

The order intake and momentum seen in the first quarter of 2026 were already seen in the 2025 numbers. While the 12-month backlog rose by a solid 7% to $8.2 billion, it really was a 23% increase in the total backlog to $27.4 billion, which was impressive.

Full Steam Ahead

After this solid year, Wabtec showed no signs of slowing down, with 2026 sales seen moving up to $12.19-$12.49 billion, up around 10%. Moreover, continued operating leverage is seen with adjusted earnings up to $10.05-$10.45 per share, up 12-16% per year.

Further, returns to investors are also seen growing with the board upping the buyback program to $1.2 billion, as dividends have been hiked by 24% to $0.31 per share, for an annual payout of $1.24 per share. While the improvements are spectacular, current payouts provide just a 0.5% dividend yield here.

The strong order intake in the first quarter provides real support in the 2026 guidance, all of which is quite encouraging and thus has been driving shares higher. This comes as Wabtec has become a giant, and after struggling a bit in the years following the GE Transportation merger, real operating momentum has been seen more recently, driven by M&A but also outright strong execution.

What Now?

Back in the fall, I believed that earnings power would only approach $10 per share in 2026, but now it becomes obvious that the company will likely top this with ease. This has boosted the shares, which have risen from levels around the $200 mark in November to $253 right now, strongly outperforming the wider market.

The share price gains outweigh the near-term boost to earnings per share, and based on the guidance for this year, shares have re-rated to 24-25 times forward earnings. That is a rather steep multiple for a company that has been trading around 20 times earnings in more recent times.

Moreover, the outlook remains good, as the societies across the globe are battling with bad traffic, providing structural advantages of rail transportation, as well as demand for transit systems as well, with that segment responsible for 30% of Wabtec's sales. While transit carries lower margins and slower growth, congestion and expensive fuel might provide medium-term drivers here.

On the back of the good news, a lot has been priced into the stock here. Trading around the $90 mark when the company announced its merger with GE Transportation in 2018, the company and thus shares really had to digest that deal, this still being a $100 stock until 2023.

Massive momentum made shares double to $200 by year-end 2024. Continued momentum was seen over the past year. This is driven by a combination of organic growth, solid capital returns in between buybacks, dividends, M&A, margin gains, and strong order intake. This makes investors feel comfortable awarding higher multiples to the business, with the runway for growth seen as longer and stronger.

That is clearly what management is guiding for, making investors upbeat here with the track record in place in recent years. For the coming five years, the company sees mid-single-digit organic sales growth, with about 350 basis points of operating margin gains seen. This creates a roadmap for earnings to advance by ten percentage points or so for years.

That is very encouraging, certainly as the North American railcar-built market is seen as soft (again) in 2026, after 2025 was a softer year already. This only accelerates the aging of active locomotives, with a quarter of locomotives in operation for 20 years or older. This, of course, drives near-term demand for maintenance, equipment, and eventually modernization efforts here.

Despite the strong run in the shares, which is fully supported by the strong performance of the business and outlook, I am leaning cautious. This strong performance has come at a time when the market at large has seen some setbacks, marking great outperformance. This makes me very appreciative of the business and the stock, and while I expect continued strength and support of the shares, I fail to see a compelling risk-reward here.

data.title: Assertio: Why I'm Selling The Rally (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Assertio Overview

My last look at Assertio (ASRT) was in September 2023. A key company product, Indocin, faced new generic competition. But Assertio was no stranger to generic entries and had a few products to offset some losses from Indocin. Back then, Assertio was growing net product sales by 13% and was cash generative.

When I wrote this piece, the stock was amid a large drop. After reaching >$100/share just months earlier, ASRT traded in the $40s (Note: there was a 1-for-15 reverse stock split in December). I rated ASRT a "Hold," but cautioned that "potential further decline" was possible "despite sparkling valuation metrics."

As is often the case in the stock market, "cheap" stocks are "cheap" for good reason and have a tendency to stay "cheap." Indeed, ASRT has fallen another 61% versus S&P 500 (SPY) gains of 46%.

Zooming in, ASRT has, however, rallied 90% in the past year.

Below, I take another look to see if there remains "wind behind Assertio's back."

Recent Performance

I always pay close attention to Seeking Alpha's Quant ratings, and ASRT ranks 32 overall out of 4266 tickers. Again, ASRT sports a sparkling "A-" grade on Valuation. Its P/E Non-GAAP (forward) is just 5.11. The sector (Health Care) median is 17.14.

ASRT also earns good scores in Growth (B; 81% YoY EBITDA Growth), Profitability (B-; Gross Profit Margin 70.19%), and Momentum (aka "stock performance").

What does this all mean?

I'd argue that a P/E of 5.11 signals that the market expects earnings to collapse further. Assertio has not been able to offset the losses of its most profitable product, Indocin.

Recall that Rolvedon was an acquired asset. It is quickly becoming "all Assertio has." Rolvedon now accounts for ~58% of total revenue. Any pricing pressure or market share loss for Rolvedon could leave Assertio with very little.

Moreover, Assertio's FY25 performance requires some nuance.

It was artificially buoyed by a "sell-in" event in Q3. The company pushed five quarters' worth of Rolvedon wholesaler shipments to manage a distribution transition. This, in large part, accounted for the "81% YoY EBITDA Growth."

Regarding the 70.19% Gross Profit Margin, this is actually relatively modest for a specialty pharma company compared to peers like Catalyst (CPRX), Harmony (HRMY), and Corcept (CORT).

Assertio's margins are also trending lower, with hopes that they have bottomed.

Then, of course, Momentum grades can simply reflect a "dead cat bounce" or temporary volatility rather than a fundamental turnaround.

Speaking of which, are there any actual signs of a turnaround?

Management's guidance did provide some early signs.

For the first time since the Indocin generic entry, management provided a clear floor for profitability. They are also busy "right-sizing" cost structures, as evidenced by diminishing SG&A expenses. Finally, the "noise" from the Spectrum acquisition is largely over. As I often point out in my analyses, it can take years to fully integrate an acquisition.

On the flip side, Assertio's cash is dwindling, GAAP net losses are accumulating ($30.4M in 2025), and further Indocin erosion is expected in 2026.

Assertio's "Altman Z Score," which, according to Seeking Alpha, "may be used to predict the probability that a firm will go into bankruptcy within two years," is -2.81, falling materially short of the "above 3" of companies that "are not likely to go bankrupt."

Assertio's below-zero score is a feature of accumulated deficit (from years of restructuring and acquisitions), operating losses, and a market capitalization (~$120M) that is less than Assertio's total liabilities ($173M).

Management Commentary

During the call, management revealed that FY26 will only reflect three quarters of Rolvedon shipments (beginning Q2). This will give the appearance of a massive YoY acceleration compared to the artificial "zero" they created in Q4 '25 and Q1 '26.

When it comes to strategy, management confirmed that on-market products are "no longer capital efficient" and that Assertio is shifting towards "development stage" assets.

In other words, the company has run out of money to buy meaningful, revenue-generating products like Rolvedon. This is a telltale sign that Assertio's operations are shifting from "specialty pharma" to "biotech speculation," albeit later-stage assets. And, of course, the latter is also associated with increasing costs (i.e., R&D).

Rolvedon

Because Assertio is essentially a "Rolvedon company with a few diminishing assets," I wanted to take the time to review how robust this franchise is.

Rolvedon, as a franchise, has some good features and some bad.

As management loves to point out, its IP extends to 2039. It has its own unique permanent J-code (J1449). And it is established in NCCN and ASCO guidelines.

The problem is that Rolvedon is just a "challenger" brand in a competitive market.

Amgen's (AMGN) Neulasta Onpro is a passive on-body injector that allows patients to go home and have the drug automatically injected 24 hours later. Whereas Rolvedon is currently a prefilled syringe that requires patients to return to the clinic the next day.

Assertio's partner, Hanmi, is counteracting with an auto-injector (still requires active patient self-administration) that is expected to launch in the 1H '26 in South Korea (Assertio has U.S. rights). But it still doesn't match the "set it and forget it" convenience of Amgen's Onpro.

Then you also have to bear in mind that there are now over half a dozen biosimilars competing on price.

Rolvedon's US market is also purposefully niche. Management opted to focus primarily on community oncology clinics.

This limits TAM. So I wouldn't expect Rolvedon to suddenly become a major growth catalyst for Assertio, even with a more convenient auto-injector eventually coming to the US (management appears to be maximizing the prefilled syringe in community clinics for now).

Financial Health

At the end of '25, Assertio had $63.4M in cash and investments.

In Q4, "cash burn" (cash from operations) was negative $30M. However, this was impacted by one-offs (i.e., working capital expansion). To ascertain "cash runway," which, to my knowledge, the company did not estimate itself, it would be more prudent to look at cash from operations in the trailing twelve months (negative $28.2M). If we divide Assertio's cash and investments by this figure, it implies a cash runway of roughly nine quarters.

The company may need to raise more funds within a couple of years, which could involve substantial dilution at current prices.

Risks

Although I've covered many Assertio-specific risks already within the body of my analysis, I'd also like to take a moment to address some microcap-specific risks.

Due to a market capitalization of just ~$120M, ASRT investors should also be aware of risks such as low liquidity, dilution, volatility, a total loss of investment, and limited public information.

Challenges to Thesis

Some things that could challenge my bearish thesis include:

  • Assertio reaching GAAP profitability
  • Rolvedon auto-injector success
  • Margin stabilization
  • Accretive M&A
  • Indocin revenue floor
  • Cash reserve growth
  • Organic EBITDA growth

Conclusion

My cautious "Hold" recommendation back in 2023 supposed that Assertio would be able to somewhat offset/slow down Indocin's LOE, but the exact opposite is happening. While Rolvedon has buoyed things to some extent, it does not appear positioned for meaningful growth in the future. And Assertio's $63.4M in cash and investments is no longer enough to immediately offset Indocin-related losses via M&A. Instead, the company has its eyes on assets still in development, which entails high clinical risk and R&D expenditures. Lastly, Assertio's sparkling Quant grades appear to be a mirage.

So, I'd use this opportunity to sell (downgrade ASRT to "Sell").

data.title: Employment Report: 178K Jobs Added In March, Better Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 178,000 jobs were added in March, up from February's 133,000 loss. This figure was better than the projected addition of 65,000 jobs and marks the largest gain since December 2024. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate inched down to 4.3%, coming in below the forecasted 4.4% rate.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 178,000 in March, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.3 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Job gains occurred in health care, in construction, and in transportation and warehousing. Federal government employment continued to decline.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.3 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.2 million, changed little in March. These measures also changed little over the year.

Establishment Survey Data:Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 178,000 in March, following a decrease in February (-133,000). In March, job gains occurred in health care, in construction, and in transportation and warehousing. Federal government employment continued to decline. Payroll employment had changed little on net over the prior 12 months.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last five years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 68,000, the highest level since April 2025.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 22,000.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over. The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.256% (to three decimal places), the lowest level since June 2025.

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.26%, lower than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.31%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 33 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but quickly fell. This rate has recently been trending upwards and now stands at 1.1%.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 25.3 weeks, down from 25.7 in February. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work. Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions.

In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks. The U6 series currently stands at 8.0%, up from 7.9% in February.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 61.9%, its lowest level since the end of 2021.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.9%, down from 7.2% in February.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. The latest reading is at 59.2%, the lowest level since the end of 2021.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fox Factory: Still Offers A Nice Long-Term Ride Despite The Tough Road
data.description:
In early October of last year, I wrote an article wherein I argued that, even though shares of Fox Factory Holding Corp. (FOXF) had underperformed the market, the company offered enough upside to justify a soft "Buy" rating. This is because of its return to revenue growth and improving operational performance on the bottom line. Since then, we have indeed seen revenue increase. But bottom-line results have been something of a mixed bag.

The market has punished the company accordingly, especially with the expectation that 2026 will show weakness for the business. Shares have fallen 28.6% since the time that article was published. By comparison, the S&P 500 is down just 5.8%. This is disappointing. However, even though revenue will be weaker this year than it was last year, cost-cutting initiatives being implemented by management should push profitability higher. And when you consider how the stock is priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other comparable firms, it's clear to me that the market has this one wrong.

An Undervalued Play

Operationally speaking, Fox Factory Holding Corp. is a business that produces and sells products and systems that are used mostly on bikes, off-road vehicles, trucks, side-by-sides, on-road vehicles, ATVs, snowmobiles, and specialty vehicles. Although it's not a core part of the business, the firm also sells premium baseball and softball gear and equipment. This is certainly a niche business. And it is one that, recently, has experienced a bit of volatility. Currently, the newest data that we have covers through the 2025 fiscal year. And what that shows is definitely a mixed picture.

In the chart above, you can see results for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. After revenue dropped from 2023 to 2024, it rebounded almost perfectly in 2025. This is great in and of itself. But I wish I could say that profitability had followed suit. The picture is actually more nuanced than that. The firm went from a net profit of $6.6 million in 2024, which was already down from the $120.8 million reported the year before that, to negative $544.6 million in 2025. The good news is that the pain seen in 2025 was the result of $557.3 million of goodwill impairments and $13.5 million of intangible and long-lived asset impairment charges. But even on an adjusted basis, profitability has been declining for at least three years now. Cash flows have been more of a mixed bag, with adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA at least rising from 2024 to 2025.

To really understand what is going on with the business, we do need to dig a bit deeper. The best place to start, I would argue, would actually be its largest operating segment. That is none other than its Specialty Sports Group segment, which focuses on the production and sale of bike suspension products, as well as its sale of baseball and softball gear and equipment. After seeing revenue surge from $389.2 million in 2023 to $511.1 million in 2024, there was only a bit of a dip to $509.2 million last year. With that decline came a drop in profits from $117.8 million to $107.6 million. That's not a big decline in the grand scheme of things. But it matters all the same. According to management, the recent weakness that the company saw was the result of lower diamond sports product sales that more than offset a rise in bike sales.

This was, unfortunately, the worst-performing part of the business. By comparison, other parts of the company performed much better. Take, if you will, its Powered Vehicles Group, which involves the company's off-road and specialty vehicle suspension components. Management did report a rise in revenue here from $461.4 million in 2024 to $488.1 million last year. Management chalked this up to greater demand for powersports and aftermarket applications. And with that rise came higher profits of $62.3 million, which compared favorably against the $53.8 million reported the previous year.

But perhaps the best part of the company, at least right now, is its Aftermarket Applications Group segment. Its name is pretty self-explanatory. And after seeing revenue plunge from 2023 to 2024, it jumped a whopping 11.5% in 2025. Increased demand for aftermarket products and a rise in upfitting sales more than offset broader economic conditions to push revenue higher. And with that, profitability improved also. The $55.8 million in segment profits is still well below what the company saw in 2023. But it did happen to be 7.9% higher than what the company saw in 2024.

Even though management ended 2025 on a high note when it came to revenue, the expectation for 2026 is rather disappointing. Currently, they are forecasting sales of between $1.328 billion and $1.416 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a drop of 6.5% year over year. Some of this is because of expected weakness in its end markets. However, most of it seems to be intentional. Management has committed to selling off certain assets. Specifically, in early January of this year, management initiated the process to sell off its Phoenix, Arizona, AAG operations, though we do not yet know what price they might get for those. On top of this, management is working to rationalize some of their other product lines. This rationalization effort is aimed at addressing margin pressures that the company has faced from tariffs, supply chain issues, and overall operational complexity.

The hope is that, between the asset sales and certain cost-cutting efforts, the company can see overall expenses drop by $50 million this year. Should this come to fruition, it would mean that EBITDA would come in at between $174 million and $203 million for the year. That compares to the $168.4 million that management captured in 2025. Assuming that this occurs as planned, that would likely mean adjusted net profits of about $49.9 million and adjusted operating cash flow amounting to $111.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see what this would mean for the valuation of the company compared to how the stock is valued today using results from 2025. Even without this bottom-line improvement, I would argue that the stock is attractively priced on an absolute basis. It's also cheap compared to most other similar firms. In that same chart, I compared it to five such businesses. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the firms was cheaper than it is. Two were cheaper when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. Meanwhile, it ended up being the cheapest of the group when it came to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach.

Takeaway

The data that is available to us right now shows what can only be described as a mixed bag. Having said that, I think that there are reasons to be optimistic here. Shares of the company look attractively priced if you ask me. But that's not all. While revenue will be disappointing this year, management's emphasis on bottom-line results is precisely what we want to see. And when you add on top of this margin improvement, plus the fact that the stock is cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, then rating it a "Buy" still makes sense in my eyes.

data.title: Apogee Enterprises: The Discount Is What Matters, Especially With Cost-Cutting
data.description:
In late December of last year, I decided to reaffirm Apogee Enterprises, Inc. (APOG), as a "Buy" candidate. I did this even though I knew that the company was facing declining profitability at a time when revenue was on the rise. I viewed margin compression as more of a short-term impact because of tariffs and an unfavorable change in product mix. In addition to the idea that profitability would be impacted in just the near term, I was also attracted to just how cheap shares were. On both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies, the stock looked very cheap. And this justified, in my view, a bullish outlook.

Since then, however, the stock has underperformed the market. It's down 11.1%, while the S&P 500 is down 8.1%. This is disappointing to say the least. And, in all honesty, it's not completely unwarranted. The most recent data that management has provided shows worsening profitability continuing. And, unfortunately, the expectation is that margin headwinds will remain problematic, pushing profits even lower this year than previously anticipated. Even with that factored in, however, I stand by my stance. Shares are so cheap that, in my view, they are worth this painful ride.

Sticking To My Guns

Since my previous article about Apogee Enterprises, investors have only been given access to data covering a single additional operating quarter. That is the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $348.6 million. That happens to be up 2.1% compared to the $341.3 million that the company reported a year earlier. This is great in and of itself. However, bottom-line results for the business have worsened. Net income declined from $21 million to $16.5 million. And even if we adjust for certain one-time events, we would get a fall from $26.1 million to $22 million. Operating cash flow plummeted from $41 million to $29.3 million. And stripping out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $42.4 million to $36.6 million. In fact, the only profitability metric to improve year over year was EBITDA. It managed to tick up from $49.3 million to $50.4 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue increased like what we saw in the third quarter on its own. But profits and cash flows worsened. This is disappointing to say the least, and it has even caused management to revise lower their expectations when it comes to the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety. Previously, management expected revenue of between $1.39 billion and $1.42 billion. That has now been changed to the low end of the range at $1.39 billion. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share are now forecasted to come in at between $3.40 and $3.50. That represents a pretty big downward revision from the $3.60 to $3.90 range that management previously anticipated. And, at the midpoint, it would mean adjusted net profits of 74.5 million. For 2025, adjusted net income was $108.9 million. This likely means a decline in certain cash flow metrics as well. For the purpose of analyzing the company, I am projecting adjusted operating cash flow of $136.8 million and EBITDA of $159.8 million.

The pain that the company has seen has been caused by different operating segments in different capacities. For instance, even though revenue is on the rise, the Architectural Metals segment has actually reported a drop in sales. The $124.4 million in revenue achieved by it in the third quarter of 2026 was well below the $138 million reported the year before that. According to management, this decline was driven entirely by organic means, with the drops specifically caused by lower volumes that more than offset higher prices and a favorable change in product mix. Even though management has been cutting costs, overall profitability contracted also, dropping from $17.5 million to $16.8 million.

Speaking of cost cutting, management is currently working on what it considers to be phase two of their Project Fortify initiative. Previously, the firm thought that this would result in annualized cost savings of between $13 million and $15 million, but that has now jumped up to between $25 million and $26 million on a pre-tax basis. This is great to see in and of itself. However, management does still expect some impact from tariffs this year. They expect that the impact on a post-tax basis of tariffs will be around $6.5 million. So that will offset some of the cost savings.

We have seen cost-cutting initiatives show improvement elsewhere for the company. As revenue grew from $104.9 million to $105.2 million year over year, the Architectural Services segment for the company reported a small uptick in profits from $10 million to $10.2 million. The rise in revenue certainly helped here, with the company attributing it to higher volumes. But when it comes to other parts of the company, we have seen weakness. The greatest example of this would be the Architectural Glass segment, which did report a rise in sales from $70.2 million to $70.9 million, but with that ultimately translating to a decline in profits from $13.2 million to $11.5 million. That drop seems to have been the result of lower prices and a rise in material costs that more than offset higher volumes and a favorable change in product mix.

The true star of the show, however, was none other than the company’s Performance Services segment. Revenue for the third quarter ended up being $53 million. That was significantly above the $33.2 million reported the previous year. And with that, segment profits jumped from $8.6 million to $11.9 million. As great as this was to see, context is important here. The rise in sales was thanks mostly to $18.4 million of additional revenue that the company got because of the firm's purchase of UW Solutions. This is a U.S.-based producer of high-performance coated substrates that the company bought up for $232.2 million. However, the good news is that this segment still did see some organic revenue growth amounting to 4.3%. So at least we aren't seeing organic weakness.

If we use the estimates that I calculated previously that were based on management's guidance for 2026, and we also use the results that management reported for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are valued at the moment. Honestly, these are really attractive multiples if you ask me. I always love a business that's trading at the mid-single-digit range from a trading multiple standpoint. Relative to other comparable firms, the stock is cheap as well. The chart above shows that none of the five companies that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. To quantify just how cheap the stock is at the moment, we need only look at the chart below. In it, you can see how much upside shares of Apogee Enterprises would experience if the business were to trade at the multiple of the cheapest of these five enterprises. Yes, there is one scenario where the stock would see an upside of only 2%. But that seems to be an outlier. More realistically, we are looking at an upside of between 32% and 47.9%. And if we instead value the company based on the average of these other comparable firms, upside jumps to between 62.7% and 190.9%.

Takeaway

I must say that I remain optimistic about Apogee Enterprises. Even though the last few months have been difficult, I see no reason not to be bullish considering how cheap shares are. The stock is certainly not the best prospect on the market. But I do view it as a potential turnaround play. At some point, market conditions will stabilize. And once we start seeing consistent organic growth across the board, significant appreciation is likely. Because of this, I believe that maintaining the company as a "Buy" candidate is the right move here.

data.title: Guardant Health: Leveraging Liquid Biopsy Testing Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis and Introduction

We are in the 2nd quarter of the year 2026, and you notice that in my previous article, my rating for Guardant Health, Inc. (GH) was bullish, driven by colorectal cancer screening. Well, the company stock price has lost around 12% since my last published article, which I consider to be due to the cyclic nature of markets. This is because the company maintains its growth aspect by recording a revenue of $281 million in Q4 ’25, representing 39% YoY growth. In terms of the Annual revenue record, the company still records $982 million in revenues, up from $739 million, representing 33%.

Therefore, I believe that this company maintains a strong bullish momentum driven by the oncology segment. To add on to my previous focus on colorectal testing, which is part of the oncology segment, is the liquid biopsy testing. Oncology is currently driving revenue in the company at 26% YoY growth of overall revenues. This is by design. For example, the liquid biopsy testing is going beyond carcinogenic genomic testing to epigenomic features, which increases actionability. As we speak, liquid biopsy testing has resulted in 85% of metastatic breast cancer patients and 89% of advanced colorectal cancer patients obtaining clinically actionable findings. I will explain this in detail later, but I think it is this optimism that is still driving GH price return at 115.75%, which still beats the S&P 500, which is at 16.08%.

But let’s get to a deeper understanding of this to unravel why I think liquid biopsy testing adds to a bullish rating of GE.

The Care Business in Liquid Biopsy

Well, let’s be pragmatic here. Imagine you have a patient, either a friend or a loved one, suffering from an illness you are unaware of, and all basic tests do not reveal any cause of the illness. I believe that your next course of action would be to consider advanced tests relating to oncology. Why? At this point in time, I believe you could be having a clue that the U.S. is recording 2 million cancer cases as of 2026 with a projected 626,140 mortalities per annum.

This might look like a reality to worry about. Still, to your surprise, I think this is not a worrying issue as such because the mortality rates are on the decline due to reduced smoking, alcohol intake, improved treatments, and earlier detection. This goes along with what the World Health Organization (WHO) states, that 4 in 10 cases of cancer can be prevented globally, going back to the issues of prevention I previously highlighted.

In my view, the latest research on core needle biopsy specifically for biopsy tumors shows that they increase the metastases, but there is no recent research that confirms these previous research findings. However, updated reports still show that tissue samples carry significant risks related to complications from the procedure itself. This is why more patients are considering liquid biopsy testing over tissue testing, as they can offer minimal risks and costs associated with traditional cancer diagnostics.

Honestly, I believe hearing that your patient can undergo non-invasive tests that aid in the best treatment outcomes would be an interesting outcome. This is exactly what GH liquid biopsy is offering to its patients, as it offers early detection, increased specificity, and sensitivity. It is from this perspective that I am looking at GH’s liquid biopsy as a solution to an existing cancer tissue testing problem that I would consider bypassing with a patient in mind.

Now looking at it from an investor’s perspective, drawing inferences from the above perspectives, the liquid biopsy market is currently growing rapidly at a CAGR of 13.91% between 2025 and 2034. The market volume is expected to hit $8.07 billion in 2026 and $22.69 billion by 2034. This is a testament to my previous assertions that liquid biopsy actionable results can be forecasted financially.

Financials and Valuation

The company still maintained an impressive top line in FY2025, as projected, by beating analysts’ estimates by a surprise of 0.98%. The estimates were $972.50 million, but the company hit $982.02 million, representing 33% YoY growth. As noted in my previous article, the company is gaining revenue upside momentum due to these new oncology tests, which are driving its revenues. This was a revenue growth YoY, up from 31.04%, and most of the revenues are generated from oncology. It is inevitable to overlook the innovation and problem solution in oncology, which is generating $190 million, representing 67.6% of the overall company revenues as of FY2025. This beat has been consistent in the last four years, and with an approximated revenue of $1.27 billion in FY2026, representing 29.44%, GH has the potential to beat this estimate. This revenue estimate is slightly above the company’s $1.25-$1.28 billion range, which falls within attainable margins.

Even from the profitability picture, you notice that despite the company investing heavily in research and development, the amount increased from $347.75 million in FY2024 to $365.19 million in FY2025. Now, the most interesting part is that the net loss still decreased from -$436.37 million in FY2024 to -$417.54 million in FY2025. In addition, I think, given the rapid growth of the liquid biopsy testing market and GH’s 360 liquid biopsy positioning, the company has the potential to achieve another improved profitability in FY2026, looking at the estimated EPS of -$1.56, representing 14.43% YoY growth. The company continues to show more potential with an EPS estimate of -$0.62 in FY2027, reaching 60.18% YoY growth.

From a valuation angle, a comparison of GH’s P/S multiple to that of its peers, BrightSpring Health Services, Inc. (BTSG) and Chemed Corporation (CHE), is a distant higher at 11.98x to its peers. BTSG is at 0.68x and CHE is at 2.17x, which is in the same range but a distant down from GH. But when I look at the forward revenue growth rate of the peers, it shows that GH is currently fairly valued. At a P/S ratio of 11.98x and a forward revenue growth rate of 30.29%, which is highest among its peers BTSG at 14.03% and CHE at 5.27%, this shows that low valuation for its peers is justified and hence is GH based on revenue growth.

Now assume this company achieves analysts’ consensus revenue estimate of $1.27 billion by the end of FY2026. At an approximate P/S ratio of 11x, I am forecasting a possible upside potential to $109.57. This is after considering an assumption that the shares outstanding might maintain an uptrend of 2%, as recorded in FY2025.

Investment Risk

Sustained Operating Losses

Despite oncology showing high potential at 26% YoY growth from the market tailwinds and increased segment research and development expenditure, the company is still recording sustained operating losses. The operating expenses rose to $904 million in FY2025, up from $757 million in FY2024. This has compressed EBITDA to negative $221 million in 2025, which is a modest improvement from negative $258 million. In my view, this is a threat to GH’s profitability sustainability.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my bullish stance driven on GH, as this time the oncology segment, though liquid biopsy testing shows potential when looking at its rapid market growth. Basically, this new way of diagnosing cancers is becoming more ideal over tissue cut testing, which is why this is becoming an ideal business proposition.

data.title: Crescent Capital BDC: Additional Downside Risks Remain (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Over the last few quarters, the business development sector has faced headwinds related to high-interest rates. Higher interest rates have put pressure on borrowers that led to concerns around non-accruals and write-downs. Furthermore, the higher cost of debt means that BDCs have struggled to find new opportunities to invest in. Unfortunately, the Crescent Capital BDC (CCAP) has been no exception to these challenges. When I previously covered CCAP, I issued a hold rating due to the weak earnings but solid dividend coverage.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CCAP's share price has declined by 26.7%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of about 16.7%. Following the decline, CCAP now offers investors a starting dividend yield of roughly 14.4%. The dividend is still covered by earnings, but the coverage is getting thin. Therefore, I remain cautious about CCAP's ability to sustain its payouts if portfolio metrics don't improve over the next few quarters.

Due to the headwinds, CCAP has fallen to one of its deepest discount-to-NAV valuations ever. Despite this, I do not think it's a good time to accumulate shares. Instead, the deep discount is a direct reflection of the headwinds that CCAP is facing. Management has struggled to invest in new opportunities, and the underlying NAV has declined for a year straight. CCAP has offset lower earnings with reduced costs, but this isn't enough to drive any meaningful improvements.

Q4 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, CCAP has total investments at fair value of $1.56B that are spread across a diverse range of debt securities. The portfolio has declined year-over-year from its prior fair value of $1.59B. While the decline is small, it also shows us that there was a lack of growth over the last twelve months. Unlike traditional equities, CCAP's success does not rely on the earnings growth of its positions. Instead, CCAP lends capital to middle-market borrowers and relies on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance.

CCAP relies most heavily on first lien debt, which is ideal since this debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure. First lien debt has the highest priority for repayment, and this reduces the probability that CCAP will lose all invested capital in a bad deal. So if a borrower needs to sell off assets, CCAP would be first in line for repayment before other forms of debt. The portfolio's structure by investment type is as follows:

  • Senior Secured First Lien: 22.4%
  • Unitranche First Lien: 66.7%
  • Equity & Other: 4.9%
  • LLC/LP Equity Interests: 2.3%
  • Unitranche First Lien – Last Out: 1.7%
  • Unsecured Debt: 1.2%
  • Senior Secured Second Lien: 0.8%

Expanding on this, CCAP also spreads its assets across different industries. CCAP is most heavily allocated to the healthcare equipment and services industry at 27% of assets. However, this is followed by a large allocation of 20% towards software and service investments. The indices have continued to sell off over the last few months due to the threat of AI. The idea is that the capabilities of AI have become so strong that it now threatens the moat that many software companies had. CCAP's exposure to software companies can also introduce some volatility since sentiment seems to be cautious at this time. However, management was able to clarify that they remain confident in their software investments on the last earnings call.

What we've seen within our software portfolio to date is that the performance has been quite strong. We're seeing both revenue and EBITDA growth across our portfolio within software as well as demonstrable deleveraging that has coincided with strong fundamental performance there. - Henry Chung, President

CCAP reported its Q4 earnings at the end of February, and the results were mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.45 per share, which was a decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.55 per share. Total investment income amounted to $40.8M, which was also a decline from the prior year's total investment income of $46.4M. The main driver of the lower earnings was the reduced interest income that CCAP was able to collect from its positions.

On a positive note, CCAP was able to slightly reduce its total operating expenses to help offset the lower earnings. Total operating expenses amounted to $24.3M, which was a small improvement from the prior year's total operating expenses of $25.9M. The biggest changes were reductions in total interest and debt financing costs and management incentive fees.

CCAP was able to capture a positive net realized gain of $3.1M during the quarter. This is notable since a high-interest rate environment tends to suppress the growth potential of a BDC's holdings. However, this was offset by net unrealized depreciation of $11.3M over the quarter. Unfortunately, depreciation of CCAP's positions has remained a challenge over the last year because of high-interest rates.

From a balance sheet perspective, there are some improvements that can be made. For instance, cash and equivalents totaled $31.4M, but the total amount of debt now totals $879.6M. CCAP does have two separate notes maturing in 2026, totaling $297M. The good news is that once this debt is tackled, CCAP has no major debt maturities until 2029. This provides the portfolio with ample time to implement improvements going forward.

Valuation and Risk Profile

When I previously covered CCAP, it traded at a discount to NAV of 22.40%. Following the continued decline in share price, CCAP now trades at a much deeper discount to NAV of 35.24%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CCAP now trades at one of its deepest discount to NAV levels ever! For reference, CCAP has traded at an average discount to NAV of 17.47% over the last five years. This deep discount can be an opportunity to accumulate, but I also believe that it's a direct reflection of the market sentiment of BDCs right now. Furthermore, I would hold off from accumulating shares as long as the NAV continues to decline.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $15.14 per share. This indicates a potential upside of ~22.4% from its current levels. However, I disagree with this optimistic outlook since there do not appear to be any meaningful catalysts on the horizon. The Fed decided to leave interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, which means that the high cost of debt will continue to limit the number of borrowers in the market seeking debt financing. Therefore, CCAP will continue to struggle to find new opportunities to invest in.

This has been an issue across the BDC sector, and CCAP is no exception. CCAP was able to allocate $70.8M towards new investments during the quarter, which is a sizeable decline from the prior year's amount of new investment activity of $127.2M. However, this was offset by sales and prepayments, resulting in a net funded investment activity of negative $7.3M for the period. As long as sales, exits, and prepayments outpace the rate of new investment activity, the underlying NAV will continue to decline since CCAP is not effectively growing its base assets or net investment income.

  • Q4 2024: $19.98 per share
  • Q1 2025: $19.62 per share
  • Q2 2025: $19.55 per share
  • Q3 2025: $19.28 per share
  • Q4 2025: $19.10 per share
